pattern: Binary search on a sorted index of start points locates the minimal start that is at least as large as each interval's end in O(n log n).
hints: For each interval you need the interval with the smallest start point that is greater than or equal to this interval's end point — a classic lower-bound binary search.
Build a sorted list of (start, original_index) pairs; for each interval, binary-search for the first start >= end and record the original index found (or -1).
Use bisect_left on a sorted starts array to find the insertion position, then map back from that start value to the original interval's index using a dictionary.

edge_inputs.description: single interval with no right neighbor
three intervals, two have right intervals
intervals where i may equal j (self-right)
end equals another interval's start exactly (inclusive boundary)
all intervals share a common end with start chain
intervals not sorted by start, right interval spans multiple options
two intervals that are each other's right interval

stress.description: large set of random non-overlapping intervals to stress binary search
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = size
    starts = rng.sample(range(0, 2 * n), n)
    intervals = []
    for s in starts:
        e = s + rng.randint(1, 5)
        intervals.append([s, e])
    return [intervals]
stress.note: unique starts guaranteed by sample; ends may exceed other starts
